questions.questionText: In acute osteomyelitis of children, the most common causative organism is:
questions.optionB: Staphylococcus aureus (most common overall cause of osteomyelitis in all age groups except neonates, where Group B Strep and E. coli predominate)
questions.explanation: Core concept: S. aureus = #1 cause of osteomyelitis in children and adults. Why correct: Hematogenous osteomyelitis in children: metaphysis of long bones (rich blood supply + sluggish flow in metaphyseal sinusoidal veins). S. aureus in >80%. Why A wrong: Strep pyogenes can cause but much less common. Why C wrong: Pseudomonas — IV drug users, puncture wounds through shoes. Why D wrong: Salmonella — characteristically in sickle cell disease patients (though S. aureus still overall more common even in SCD). Clinical relevance: Brodie abscess = chronic osteomyelitis localized abscess (usually tibial metaphysis). Involucrum = new bone formed around dead bone (sequestrum). Treatment: IV antibiotics (4-6 weeks) + surgical drainage if needed. Exam trap: Sickle cell disease: Salmonella osteomyelitis is CHARACTERISTIC but S. aureus is still MORE COMMON. A subtle but important distinction. Memory aid: 'S. aureus = Standard cause of osteomyelitis (all ages); Salmonella = Sickle cell (characteristic but not most common).' Reference: Maheshwari – Bone Infections.
questions.conceptTag: Osteomyelitis S. aureus metaphysis children Salmonella SCD
